text: Minimal adhesions between the lung and the chest wall were taken down.
Multiple gauzes soaked with scolicidal hypertonic saline was inserted into the chest cavity.
and then we proceeded with adhesolysis and maturation of the fused fissure.
The lower edge of the left upper lobe was fused to the cyst and the left lower lobe and that was taken down by combination of electrocautery and blunt dissection.
The dissection and the adhesolysis
were continued down to the fissure hilum to separate the cyst from the hilum structures.
A metallic needle was inserted into the chest cavity and introduced into the cyst cavity which was followed by aspiration of the fluid from the cyst.
Then, hypertonic saline was injected into the cyst to fill it completely and act as a scolicidal agent.
This process of aspirating fluid and distillation of hypertonic saline was performed several times.

After completely aspirating the hypertonic saline after multiple irrigation sessions, we continued with the adhesolysis to completely separate the collapsed cyst from the surrounding structures, mainly the lower edge of the left upper lobe.
Then, using the harmonic scalpel, we created a small incision in the cyst wall to get access to the inside of the cyst cavity.
Scissor was utilized to expand and enlarge this small incision.
It was immediately obvious that there was a germinative layer inside with the remaining fluid from the hypertonic saline irrigation.
We continue aspirating what's remaining of the hypertonic saline and the germinative layer was taken out in one piece outside the chest cavity.
We then performed partial resection of the outer cyst layer using the harmonic scalpel to gain access to the inside of the cyst cavity.
This will allow us to inspect the base of the cavity and examine for air leaks coming from any small bronchial stumps.
To identify the areas of the air leak, we instilled the cyst cavity with hypertonic saline and we asked the anesthesia team to gently inflate the left lung.
We were able to immediately identify a couple of air leak places from the base of the cyst.
The hypertonic saline was then aspirated and we proceeded with suture closure of the bronchial stumps and performing capitonage of the cyst cavity to occlude it.
After performing the capitonage, we asked the anesthesia team again to gently inflate the lung to test for any air leaks.
There were no air leaks after the capitonage.
We then redeflated the lung and proceeded with multilevel intercostal nerve blocks at the level of the incision, two spaces above and two spaces below.
A single chest tube was inserted and then the wound was closed in multiple layers.
The patient recovered uneventfully and was discharged home on postoperative day number three with no air leaks.
